An introduction to who I am, what I build, and why I build it — from dark aesthetics to WebGL and beyond.

I go by bl0xsmith. I'm a programmer who builds things for the web — not just functional things, but things that feel alive.

My work sits at the intersection of engineering and visual craft. I care about performance and clean code, but I also care about what something *looks* like in motion, how a particle dissolves, how a glow pulses in rhythm with an interaction. That tension between precision and expression is where I spend most of my time.

## Where I'm Coming From

I started out like most developers — building UIs, chasing bugs, learning the fundamentals. But somewhere along the way I became obsessed with the visual layer. Not in a shallow sense, but technically: what the GPU is actually doing, how shaders work, why some animations feel smooth and others feel cheap.

Dark themes weren't a style choice for me — they became a constraint that pushed me to be more intentional. When you strip away the noise of a white background, every element has to earn its place. Color becomes a signal. Motion becomes meaning.

## What I Build

Most of my work falls into a few categories:

**Creative web experiences** — Three.js particle systems, WebGL shaders, interactive 3D elements that run in the browser at 60fps. I like the challenge of making GPU-driven visuals that are also practical and accessible.

**Dark UI systems** — Carefully designed interfaces built on tight color palettes, purposeful typography, and animation that responds to the user rather than just playing at them.

**Tools and experiments** — CLI utilities, theme packages, shader sandboxes. Smaller projects I build because I want them to exist.

## Stack

Day to day I work with TypeScript, Astro, Three.js, and GLSL. I lean on the web platform as much as possible — the less framework overhead, the better. Performance is non-negotiable; anything that ships to a browser should be fast.

## What This Space Is

This blog is where I'll write about what I'm building and what I'm learning. Shader techniques, animation patterns, design decisions, the occasional deep dive into something I had to figure out the hard way. No content calendar, no SEO optimization — just things worth writing down.

If you want to see what I'm working on, the [projects](/projects) page is the place to start.

A personal CLI for scaffolding dark-themed Astro and web projects from zero to running in under a minute

`bl0x-cli` is a command-line tool that bootstraps my standard project setup — Astro + TypeScript + dark theme config + opinionated folder structure — in a single command.

## Usage

```bash
npx bl0x-cli new my-project
cd my-project
npm run dev
```

## What Gets Scaffolded

- Astro project with TypeScript strict mode
- Pre-configured `tsconfig.json` and `astro.config.mjs`
- Global CSS with dark-theme custom properties already wired
- Three.js installed and a starter `ThreeBackground` component
- Content collections for `blog` and `projects`
- Git initialized with a sensible `.gitignore`

## Templates

| Template | Description |
|---|---|
| `default` | Blog + portfolio (what you're looking at) |
| `minimal` | Just the dark base, no content collections |
| `landing` | Single-page with hero, features, CTA sections |

## Why

I kept repeating the same setup steps for every new project. Now it's one command.

A generative art tool built on the Canvas API — design algorithmic visuals and export them as high-resolution PNGs

Dark Canvas is a tool for making generative art in the browser. Pick an algorithm, dial in the parameters, hit generate, and export — all on a black canvas.

## Algorithms Included

- **Flow Field** — particles follow a Perlin noise vector field
- **Voronoi** — colored cell diagrams with adjustable seed count
- **L-System** — fractal plant/tree structures from grammar rules
- **Recursive Subdivisions** — Mondrian-style geometric layouts
- **Circle Packing** — fills a space with non-overlapping circles
- **Strange Attractors** — Lorenz, Clifford, and De Jong

## What You Can Control

Each algorithm exposes its own set of sliders and toggles: color palette, iteration count, line weight, randomness seed, and canvas dimensions. Changing the seed regenerates the output while keeping all other settings.

## Export

Export as a PNG at up to 4× the screen resolution — suitable for printing or use as a wallpaper. The canvas dimensions and DPI multiplier are configurable before export.

## Why Dark Canvas

I wanted a place to experiment with generative algorithms without writing boilerplate every time. Dark Canvas is that scratchpad — built once, used constantly.

A heavily customized dark Astro theme featuring 5000 Three.js particles, glitch text, and the View Transitions API

The theme you're currently viewing. Darkness is an experimental Astro theme built around the idea that a portfolio should feel like an experience — not just a page.

## Features

- 5000 Three.js particles rendered in 3D space
- Custom cursor with interactive hover effects
- Glitch text animations using CSS custom properties
- Smooth page transitions via the View Transitions API
- Full TypeScript support throughout
- Blog and projects collection via Astro Content Collections
- Zero-JS fallback for core content

## Design Philosophy

The aesthetic is intentional — deep blacks, neon accents, and motion that responds to the user. Every animation is purposeful: particles drift, text glitches on entry, and transitions carry context between pages.

## Tech Stack

Built with Astro for near-zero JS overhead at the framework level, Three.js for the 3D particle canvas, and vanilla TypeScript for all interaction logic. No CSS framework — every style is hand-written.

An interactive Three.js sandbox for designing and tweaking particle systems in real time

A browser-based sandbox for building and experimenting with Three.js particle systems — no setup, no build step, just open and tweak.

## What You Can Do

Dial in particle count (up to 50,000), color gradients, spawn shapes, velocity curves, and gravity with live sliders. Every change reflects instantly on the canvas. When you land on a configuration you like, export it as a JSON preset or grab a screenshot of the current frame.

## Highlights

- Real-time parameter adjustment with zero lag
- Multiple emitter shapes: sphere, box, cone, disc
- Physics options: gravity, turbulence, drag
- Color palette picker with gradient support
- Preset export / import as JSON
- High-res screenshot capture
- Works entirely in the browser — no server required

## Why I Built It

I kept copy-pasting particle config code between projects. This turns that iteration loop into a visual tool. Design the system here, export the config, drop it into any Three.js project.

A minimal, pitch-black VS Code color theme built for long coding sessions in the dark

Void is a VS Code color theme that leans into true black backgrounds, muted syntax colors, and neon accents only where they matter. No gradients, no noise — just the code.

## Design Goals

Most dark themes are dark grey, not dark. Void uses `#000000` as the base and builds up from there with carefully chosen token colors that reduce eye strain during long sessions without sacrificing readability.

## Color Palette

- Background: `#000000`
- Surface: `#0a0a0a`
- Comments: desaturated purple-grey
- Strings: soft green
- Keywords: neon violet
- Functions: muted cyan
- Errors: deep red — visible but not aggressive

## Installation

Search **"Void"** by bl0xsmith in the VS Code Extensions marketplace, or install via the CLI:

```bash
code --install-extension bl0xsmith.void-theme
```

A live GLSL fragment shader editor that runs entirely in the browser — write, preview, and share shaders instantly

Shader Lab is a browser-based GLSL fragment shader editor. Write shader code on the left, see it render in real time on the right. No backend, no accounts — just open the URL and start coding.

## Features

- Split-pane editor with live preview (updates as you type)
- Built-in uniforms: `u_time`, `u_resolution`, `u_mouse`
- Error highlighting with inline GLSL diagnostics
- Gallery of starter shaders to learn from or remix
- Shareable URLs — the shader is encoded in the hash, no server needed
- Export current frame as a PNG

## Built-in Uniforms

```glsl
uniform float u_time;       // seconds elapsed
uniform vec2  u_resolution; // canvas dimensions in pixels
uniform vec2  u_mouse;      // mouse position, normalized 0–1
```

## Why GLSL in the Browser

The GPU is right there. Running shaders in WebGL means instant iteration with no compile step, and sharing is just a URL copy. It's the fastest way to experiment with procedural visuals.
